tossups.question: A composer with this first name introduced laudi spirituali to the Oratorians. A composer with this first name included a descending soprano solo before a chorus of “Alleluia” in his In Ecclesiis. Knud Jeppesen (“ka-nood yep-ess-en”) analyzed the dissonance of a composer with this first name. Like his student Heinrich Schütz, a composer with this first name created a set of Sacrae Symphoniae, which employed the “polychoral style” of his (*) Venetian School. This is the first name of two early Italian composers named Animuccia and Gabrieli. A composer with this first name composed over 250 motets and 140 madrigals and, according to Giuseppe Baini, saved polyphony from the Council of Trent. For 10 points, give this first name of the composer of the Pope Marcellus Mass, who was from Palestrina.
tossups.question_sanitized: A composer with this first name introduced laudi spirituali to the Oratorians. A composer with this first name included a descending soprano solo before a chorus of "Alleluia" in his In Ecclesiis. Knud Jeppesen ("ka-nood yep-ess-en") analyzed the dissonance of a composer with this first name. Like his student Heinrich Schutz, a composer with this first name created a set of Sacrae Symphoniae, which employed the "polychoral style" of his (*) Venetian School. This is the first name of two early Italian composers named Animuccia and Gabrieli. A composer with this first name composed over 250 motets and 140 madrigals and, according to Giuseppe Baini, saved polyphony from the Council of Trent. For 10 points, give this first name of the composer of the Pope Marcellus Mass, who was from Palestrina.
tossups.answer: Giovanni [accept Giovanni Animuccia; accept Giovanni Gabrieli; accept Giovanni Pierluigi da Palestrina]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Giovanni [accept Giovanni Animuccia; accept Giovanni Gabrieli; accept Giovanni Pierluigi da Palestrina]

tossups.question: A piece by this composer emphasizes the word “dominum” through homorhythmic repetition before a triple-time Alleluja section. Since 2012, The Sixteen have released nine albums dedicated to this composer’s music. The head-motif of an ascending fourth followed by a stepwise descent down appears in a piece by this composer that adds a seventh voice in its second Agnus Dei movement. In a description of this composer’s style, leaps are countered by stepwise movement in the other direction and dissonances fall on weak beats, as taught in Joseph Fux’s (“FOOKS’s”)Gradus ad Parnassum. This composer’s most famous missa sine nomine apocryphally “saved polyphony” at the Council of Trent. For 10 points, name this Italian Renaissance composer of the Pope Marcellus Mass.
tossups.question_sanitized: A piece by this composer emphasizes the word "dominum" through homorhythmic repetition before a triple-time Alleluja section. Since 2012, The Sixteen have released nine albums dedicated to this composer's music. The head-motif of an ascending fourth followed by a stepwise descent down appears in a piece by this composer that adds a seventh voice in its second Agnus Dei movement. In a description of this composer's style, leaps are countered by stepwise movement in the other direction and dissonances fall on weak beats, as taught in Joseph Fux's ("FOOKS's")Gradus ad Parnassum. This composer's most famous missa sine nomine apocryphally "saved polyphony" at the Council of Trent. For 10 points, name this Italian Renaissance composer of the Pope Marcellus Mass.
tossups.answer: Giovanni Pierluigi da Palestrina(The first line refers to his O Magnum Mysterium.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Giovanni Pierluigi da Palestrina(The first line refers to his O Magnum Mysterium.)

tossups.question: The Sanctus from Anton Bruckner’s Mass in E Minor quotes a theme by this composer, who was credited with producing the new “gradual” promoted by the Cecilian Movement. This composer put long melismas on the word desiderat (de-SEE-de-raht) in a motet based on Psalm 42, which compares the soul to a deer thirsting for water. This composer of Sicut Cervus is visited by angels and the spirits of dead composers in a “musical legend” composed in 1917. The master (*) Aloysius teaches principles based on this composer’s style in Johann Joseph Fux’s (FOOKS-’s) Gradus ad Parnassum. Hans Pfitzner wrote an opera about a mass by this composer written to ensure that listeners could understand the words. For 10 points, name this Renaissance composer who apocryphally saved polyphony with his Pope Marcellus Mass.
tossups.question_sanitized: The Sanctus from Anton Bruckner's Mass in E Minor quotes a theme by this composer, who was credited with producing the new "gradual" promoted by the Cecilian Movement. This composer put long melismas on the word desiderat (de-SEE-de-raht) in a motet based on Psalm 42, which compares the soul to a deer thirsting for water. This composer of Sicut Cervus is visited by angels and the spirits of dead composers in a "musical legend" composed in 1917. The master (*) Aloysius teaches principles based on this composer's style in Johann Joseph Fux's (FOOKS-'s) Gradus ad Parnassum. Hans Pfitzner wrote an opera about a mass by this composer written to ensure that listeners could understand the words. For 10 points, name this Renaissance composer who apocryphally saved polyphony with his Pope Marcellus Mass.

tossups.question: One piece by this composer is characterised by the recurring motif of a rising fourth followed by a stepwise descent. This composer’s first publication was modelled on that of Cristóbal de Morale, while Missa Tu es Petrus is one of this composer’s many parody masses. Appointed by Julius III to direct the Cappella Giulia, this composer said ‘I blush and grieve’ over their four collections of secular madrigals. The position that dissonance should normally fall on weak beats is derived from this composer’s works in Johann Fux’s Gradus ad Parnassum. For 10 points, name this Italian composer of polyphonic works including the Mass for Pope Marcellus.
tossups.question_sanitized: One piece by this composer is characterised by the recurring motif of a rising fourth followed by a stepwise descent. This composer's first publication was modelled on that of Cristobal de Morale, while Missa Tu es Petrus is one of this composer's many parody masses. Appointed by Julius III to direct the Cappella Giulia, this composer said 'I blush and grieve' over their four collections of secular madrigals. The position that dissonance should normally fall on weak beats is derived from this composer's works in Johann Fux's Gradus ad Parnassum. For 10 points, name this Italian composer of polyphonic works including the Mass for Pope Marcellus.

tossups.question: This composer’s Stabat Mater, a motet scored for unaccompanied Double Chorus, may have been written for Pope Gregory XIV. One treatise on counterpoint is presented as a dialogue between Aloysius, who represents this composer, and the student Josephus; that textbook, which promoted this composer’s compositional style, is the Gradus ad Parnassum by Johann Joseph Fux (“fooks”). This composer wrote a cycle of 29 motets based on excerpts from the Song of Solomon. In an opera [emphasize] about this composer, he initially refuses to take a commission from Cardinal Borromeo, who then imprisons him. This composer’s most famous piece is a missa sin nomine (“MEE-sah seen NOH-mee-nay”) that, according to legend, convinced the Council of Trent not to ban polyphony from worship. For 10 points, name this Italian Renaissance composer of the Pope Marcellus Mass.

packet.name: I (OSU A, Rice A, and Yale B)

tossups.question: This composer’s revival in 19th-century Germany was analyzed in a book by James Garratt about [this composer] and the German Romantic Imagination. The Benedictus only uses three of four voices in a mass by this composer that sets a Gregorian chant used as a matins hymn, the Missa Aeterna Christi Munera. A mass by this composer “without a name” was arranged for orchestra by J. S. Bach. This composer wrote two different masses, one for fives voice and one for four, based on (*) “L’homme arme.” [LOAM ar-MAY] The term “species counterpoint” was introduced in reference to attempts to imitate this composer’s style. All but the Agnus Dei of this composer’s most famous mass are scored for six voices. This composer’s work was the basis of Johann Fux’s textbook Gradus ad Parnassum. For 10 points, name this composer of the Pope Marcellus Mass.
tossups.question_sanitized: This composer's revival in 19th-century Germany was analyzed in a book by James Garratt about [this composer] and the German Romantic Imagination. The Benedictus only uses three of four voices in a mass by this composer that sets a Gregorian chant used as a matins hymn, the Missa Aeterna Christi Munera. A mass by this composer "without a name" was arranged for orchestra by J. S. Bach. This composer wrote two different masses, one for fives voice and one for four, based on (*) "L'homme arme." [LOAM ar-MAY] The term "species counterpoint" was introduced in reference to attempts to imitate this composer's style. All but the Agnus Dei of this composer's most famous mass are scored for six voices. This composer's work was the basis of Johann Fux's textbook Gradus ad Parnassum. For 10 points, name this composer of the Pope Marcellus Mass.

tossups.question: This man joined with Annibale Zoilo in a failed attempt to restore the plainsong. This composer’s first published book of masses landed him a position as music director at the Julian Chapel, and he also served in the papal choir until he was removed for being married. This composer of the Mass With N Name wrote O Magnum Mysterium and over three hundred other (*) motets. His most influential ideas were that dissonances should only be on passing notes and weak beats, music should have small leaps between notes counterbalanced by stepwise movement in the opposite direction, and music should not be static. For 10 points, name this composer who was important to Renaissance polyphony, which he demonstrated in his Pope Marcellus Mass.
tossups.question_sanitized: This man joined with Annibale Zoilo in a failed attempt to restore the plainsong. This composer's first published book of masses landed him a position as music director at the Julian Chapel, and he also served in the papal choir until he was removed for being married. This composer of the Mass With N Name wrote O Magnum Mysterium and over three hundred other (*) motets. His most influential ideas were that dissonances should only be on passing notes and weak beats, music should have small leaps between notes counterbalanced by stepwise movement in the opposite direction, and music should not be static. For 10 points, name this composer who was important to Renaissance polyphony, which he demonstrated in his Pope Marcellus Mass.

tossups.question: This man resigned from the St Lateran church, for which he had written some Lamentations, after a dispute over the feeding of his choirboys. In a book titled Gradus ad Parnassum, Johann Joseph Fux formalized this man’s rules for counterpoint. This man celebrated in a Hans Pfitzner opera wrote a work for six voices that has a two-part Agnus Dei with intelligible words; he wrote that work to convince the Council of Trent not to ban polyphonic music. For 10 points, name this Italian Renaissance composer of the Pope Marcellus Mass.
tossups.question_sanitized: This man resigned from the St Lateran church, for which he had written some Lamentations, after a dispute over the feeding of his choirboys. In a book titled Gradus ad Parnassum, Johann Joseph Fux formalized this man's rules for counterpoint. This man celebrated in a Hans Pfitzner opera wrote a work for six voices that has a two-part Agnus Dei with intelligible words; he wrote that work to convince the Council of Trent not to ban polyphonic music. For 10 points, name this Italian Renaissance composer of the Pope Marcellus Mass.

bonuses.leadin: This composer names a graphical interface front-end for the engraving software LilyPond. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This composer names a graphical interface front-end for the engraving software LilyPond. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Ferrara-born composer who was celebrated by composers like Pachelbel and Purcell for his early organ collection Fiori Musicali.

Parts of the Fiori Musicali were enshrined in a book of counterpoint by Johann Joseph Fux with this title. Debussy prepended “Doctor” to this title to parody exercise books by Clementi and Czerny (“CHAIR-nee”).
Though influenced more by Frescobaldi in his own music, Fux most fervently admired this earlier Italian composer who supposedly “saved polyphony” with his Pope Marcellus Mass.
parts_sanitized: Name this Ferrara-born composer who was celebrated by composers like Pachelbel and Purcell for his early organ collection Fiori Musicali.

Parts of the Fiori Musicali were enshrined in a book of counterpoint by Johann Joseph Fux with this title. Debussy prepended "Doctor" to this title to parody exercise books by Clementi and Czerny ("CHAIR-nee").
Though influenced more by Frescobaldi in his own music, Fux most fervently admired this earlier Italian composer who supposedly "saved polyphony" with his Pope Marcellus Mass.
answers: Gradus ad parnassum [or “Steps to Parnassus”; accept “Doctor Gradus ad parnassum”]
answers_sanitized: Gradus ad parnassum [or "Steps to Parnassus"; accept "Doctor Gradus ad parnassum"]

bonuses.leadin: A soprano with the first name Settimia (“sate-TEE-mee-ah”) and this last name likely broke off from the Medici concerto delle donne (“kohn-CHAIR-toh day-lay DOHN-nay”) to form a separate singing ensemble with her family. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: A soprano with the first name Settimia ("sate-TEE-mee-ah") and this last name likely broke off from the Medici concerto delle donne ("kohn-CHAIR-toh day-lay DOHN-nay") to form a separate singing ensemble with her family. For 10 points each:
parts: Give this last name of Francesca, whose La liberazione di Ruggiero (“lah lee-bay-rahtz-YOH-nay dee ruh-JAY-roh”) is the earliest known opera written by a woman.

Francesca and Settimia’s father, Giulio, was one of the first composers to use this notation in which the lowest musical instrumental line is annotated with numbers that indicate what intervals to add.

Giulio Caccini’s music failed to become popular in Rome, which was still in the grips of the super-consonant style of this Italian Renaissance composer whose Pope Marcellus Mass apocryphally “saved polyphony” (“puh-LIF-uh-nee”).
parts_sanitized: Give this last name of Francesca, whose La liberazione di Ruggiero ("lah lee-bay-rahtz-YOH-nay dee ruh-JAY-roh") is the earliest known opera written by a woman.

Francesca and Settimia's father, Giulio, was one of the first composers to use this notation in which the lowest musical instrumental line is annotated with numbers that indicate what intervals to add.

Giulio Caccini's music failed to become popular in Rome, which was still in the grips of the super-consonant style of this Italian Renaissance composer whose Pope Marcellus Mass apocryphally "saved polyphony" ("puh-LIF-uh-nee").
answers_sanitized: Caccini ("kah-CHEE-nee") [accept Francesca Caccini; accept Settimia Caccini]
figured bass [or thoroughbass; or basso cifrato; reject "basso continuo"]

bonuses.leadin: In one cycle, a piece in this genre states "Behold, man, how I suffer for you" and centers on the crucified Christ confronting Peter's betrayal. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this polyphonic sacred vocal genre, an example of which concludes the Lagrime di San Pietro in contrast to 20 secular madrigals.

The Lagrime di San Pietro and the motet series Prophetiae Sibyllarum were composed by Orlande de Lassus, whose style drew from this Italian Renaissance composer of a missa sin nomine that led to a debate during the Council of Trent.
The introduction to de Lassus's Prophetiae Sibyllarum is titled for a "Carmina" described by this term. Carlo Gesualdo's "Moro, lasso" employs a "direct" style described by this term.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In one cycle, a piece in this genre states "Behold, man, how I suffer for you" and centers on the crucified Christ confronting Peter's betrayal. For 10 points each:

bonuses.leadin: The so-called “first published black composer” was from this country. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this home country of a winner of a 1551 theory debate with Nicola Vincentino. The motet “Crux fidelis,” which is often performed during Holy Week, is classically misattributed to a ruler from this country.

John IV of Portugal, who didn’t actually compose “Crux fidelis,” did in fact write an essay defending this other composer. A work by this composer legendarily prevented the ban of polyphony during the Council of Trent.

Perhaps the best-known early Portuguese composer is Marcos Portugal, who is best known for his works in this genre. Claudio Monteverdi’s L’Orfeo is the earliest example of these works still regularly performed.
answers_sanitized: Portugal [or Portuguese Republic] (The composer is Vicente Lusitano.)
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The so-called "first published black composer" was from this country. For 10 points each:

Perhaps the best-known early Portuguese composer is Marcos Portugal, who is best known for his works in this genre. Claudio Monteverdi's L'Orfeo is the earliest example of these works still regularly performed.

packet.name: D – Cambridge B, Rutgers A, Waterloo A, Yale A

bonuses.leadin: Singers in an opera by Hans Pfitzner titled for this composer declare him the “savior of music.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this composer of the Pope Marcellus Mass.
Palestrina’s Pope Marcellus Mass apocryphally saved this style from being banned at the Council of Trent. The use of multiple independent voices distinguishes this musical texture from monophony.
This music theorist fictionalized himself as a student of Aloysius, an allegory of Palestrina, in a treatise that divides counterpoint into five species, Gradus ad Parnassum.
answers_sanitized: polyphony [or word forms such as polyphonic]

parts_sanitized: Name this composer of the Pope Marcellus Mass.
Palestrina's Pope Marcellus Mass apocryphally saved this style from being banned at the Council of Trent. The use of multiple independent voices distinguishes this musical texture from monophony.
This music theorist fictionalized himself as a student of Aloysius, an allegory of Palestrina, in a treatise that divides counterpoint into five species, Gradus ad Parnassum.

bonuses.leadin: The only surviving music of cantigas from this kingdom are by Martin Codax and his king. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this kingdom ruled by the "poet king" Denis. Though frequently nicknamed for Spain, "La Folia" originated in this neighboring kingdom.

King John IV wrote a defense of the style of this Italian composer popularized by Johann Fux's counterpoint treatise Gradus ad Parnassum. A legend about this composer provides the plot of a Hans Pfitzner opera.

Portugal's foremost "golden age" polyphonists were John IV, Duarte Lobo, and Manuel Cardoso, who experimented with this other texture of call-and-response music. This texture of Tomas Luis de Victoria's experiments with polychoral style is named for chants sung between verses of psalms.
answers_sanitized: Kingdom of Portugal [or Reino de Portugal]
antiphony [or word forms such as antiphonal]

bonuses.leadin: The melody of this song in 3/4 time begins [read slowly] “G, G, up to C, C, B-flat, A, G.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this medieval song whose lyrics state that its title personage “should be feared,” and whose melody was used as a cantus firmus in many settings of religious texts.

This man’s two masses based on “L’Homme Armé” include one subtitled “super voces musicales.” This man wrote Missa Pange Lingua, and was influenced by Johannes Ockeghem (“OCK-uh-ghem”) as part of the Franco-Flemish School.

Seventy years after Josquin, this composer wrote his own “L’Homme Armé” mass. This man is most famous for his “Pope Marcellus” Mass, which, according to legend, saved polyphonic music from being banned.
answers: Josquin des Prez (“joe-SCAN deh PRAY”) [or Josquin des Prez]

packet.name: E - Claremont A, JHU A, Liberty A, Toronto B, Northwestern B, Vanderbilt B, Illinois A

answers_sanitized: Josquin des Prez ("joe-SCAN deh PRAY") [or Josquin des Prez]

bonuses.leadin: Most modern performers use Glenn Watkins’s edition when performing this composer’s Tenebrae Responsories. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Italian composer whose madrigal “Moro, lasso, al mio duolo” [MOH-roh LAH-soh ahl myoh DWOH-loh] opens with the following chords in whole notes: [read slowly] “C-sharp major [pause] A minor [pause] B major [pause] D major.”

Gesualdo wrote several pieces for the Ferrarese concerto delle donne, whose style anticipated this era of European music. This term is used for music between around 1600 and 1750, including Vivaldi and Pachelbel.

This Italian Renaissance composer wrote over 140 madrigals, even after he claimed to renounce secular music in the preface to his Song of Songs. One of his masses is named for a pope who allegedly planned to ban polyphony.
answers: Giovanni Pierluigi da Palestrina (That’s the Pope Marcellus Mass.)

bonuses.leadin: Some scholars have hypothesized that this song’s titular character may be St. Michael the Archangel. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this secular song that was used as a cantus firmus for over 40 ordinary masses. Josquin des Prez notably used this song as a setting for two of his masses, one of which features an Agnus Dei in crab canon arrangement.

The earliest use of L’Homme Armé as a cantus firmus may have been by this Burgundian school composer who wrote the ballad Resvellies Vous to celebrate the marriage of Carlo Malatesta and Vittoria Colonna.

The use of secular songs such as L’Homme Armé as cantus firmus alarmed church leaders during Council of Trent. In an apocryphal story, this composer’s Pope Marcellus Mass ended up “saving” polyphony during the Council of Trent.

bonuses.leadin: In opposition to “authentic,” this term describes the even-numbered, “hypo-” prefixed church modes, whose final lies in the middle of the range. For 10 points each:
parts: Give this term for a cadence in which a 4 chord resolves to a 1 chord. It is often also called an “Amen cadence.”

A plagal cadence ends In Ecclesiis (“een ek-KLAY-see-eese”) from this organist’s 1615 Sacrae symphoniae. He composed in the polychoral style at St. Mark’s Basilica in Venice and was one of the first composers to specify instrumentation and dynamics.

This composer featured unisons, word painting, and a strong plagal cadence in a six-part motet, the first setting of O Magnum Mysterium. His Pope Marcellus Mass supposedly “saved polyphony” from condemnation at Trent.
answers_sanitized: plagal [accept plagal cadence or plagal mode]

bonuses.leadin: Like Thomas Tallis, this composer created two settings of the Lamentations of Jeremiah. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this Roman School composer who, at the commission of Urban VIII, set Psalm 51 in a piece titled Miserere mei, deus for use in the Sistine Chapel.
This composer purportedly transcribed Allegri’s Miserere from memory after a single trip to the Vatican. His own masses include the “Great Mass” in C Minor and the “Coronation” Mass in C major.
This more famous Roman School composer composed the Pope Marcellus Mass which did not, in fact, rescue polyphony in the eyes of the clergy at the Council of Trent. The legend lives on, though.

bonuses.leadin: Each movement of this composer’s Missa L’homme armé super voces musicales (MIS-uh lum ar-MAY su-PAYRE voh-KES mu-SEE-call-uh) sets the cantus firmus one note higher than in the previous movement. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this composer whose Missa pange lingua (MIS-uh PAN-jay LEEN-gwah) incorporates the title hymn into all four voices, rather than just in the tenor.
Josquin’s “Ave Maria … virgo serena” is one of these sacred choral pieces that set multiple related texts over a cantus firmus in the tenor.
This Italian Renaissance composer wrote the Pope Marcellus Mass, whose streamlined polyphonic texture is said to have convinced the Council of Trent to not ban polyphony.
answers_sanitized: Josquin des Prez [or Josquin des Prez]
Giovanni Palestrina [or Giovanni Pierluigi da Palestrina]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Each movement of this composer's Missa L'homme arme super voces musicales (MIS-uh lum ar-MAY su-PAYRE voh-KES mu-SEE-call-uh) sets the cantus firmus one note higher than in the previous movement. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this composer whose Missa pange lingua (MIS-uh PAN-jay LEEN-gwah) incorporates the title hymn into all four voices, rather than just in the tenor.
Josquin's "Ave Maria ... virgo serena" is one of these sacred choral pieces that set multiple related texts over a cantus firmus in the tenor.
This Italian Renaissance composer wrote the Pope Marcellus Mass, whose streamlined polyphonic texture is said to have convinced the Council of Trent to not ban polyphony.

bonuses.leadin: Beethoven suggested that its “four rules” could be reduced to two – one for motion into perfect consonances, and another for imperfect consonances. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this treatise and textbook by Johann Fux, mostly presented as a dialogue between the student Josephus and the teacher Aloysius. In it, Fux discusses the five species of counterpoint, as well as the proper method by which to compose for four voices.
The character of Aloysius in Gradus is based on this Italian composer of the Pope Marcellus Mass.

One of the few ways in which Fux permits the composer to leap out of a dissonance is this figure, in which a consonance moves stepwise to a dissonance, and then leaps by a third in the same direction, usually to another consonance.
answers: nota cambiata [or changed note or exchanged note; prompt on “escape” tone or note]
answers_sanitized: Gradus ad Parnassum [or Steps to Parnassus]
nota cambiata [or changed note or exchanged note; prompt on "escape" tone or note]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Beethoven suggested that its "four rules" could be reduced to two - one for motion into perfect consonances, and another for imperfect consonances. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this treatise and textbook by Johann Fux, mostly presented as a dialogue between the student Josephus and the teacher Aloysius. In it, Fux discusses the five species of counterpoint, as well as the proper method by which to compose for four voices.
The character of Aloysius in Gradus is based on this Italian composer of the Pope Marcellus Mass.

One of the few ways in which Fux permits the composer to leap out of a dissonance is this figure, in which a consonance moves stepwise to a dissonance, and then leaps by a third in the same direction, usually to another consonance.

bonuses.leadin: This man's most famous work is divided into two major sections called "Pars Speculativa" and "Pars Activa," the latter of which describes the mathematical relationships behind musical intervals. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this Austrian baroque composer and music theorist who wrote the book Gradus ad Parnassum , as well as operas like the Colossal Baroque work Costanza e Fortezza.

Fux coined this term for strict contrapuntal exercises of five increasing degrees of complexity. The first one sets one note against each note in the cantus firmus, while higher ones allowed shorter notes and suspensions.
Gradus ad Parnassum codified the contrapuntal style of this Italian composer of the free-composed Missa Papae Marcellis, which supposedly convinced the Council of Trent not to ban polyphonic music.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This man's most famous work is divided into two major sections called "Pars Speculativa" and "Pars Activa," the latter of which describes the mathematical relationships behind musical intervals. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Identify this Austrian baroque composer and music theorist who wrote the book Gradus ad Parnassum , as well as operas like the Colossal Baroque work Costanza e Fortezza.

Fux coined this term for strict contrapuntal exercises of five increasing degrees of complexity. The first one sets one note against each note in the cantus firmus, while higher ones allowed shorter notes and suspensions.
Gradus ad Parnassum codified the contrapuntal style of this Italian composer of the free-composed Missa Papae Marcellis, which supposedly convinced the Council of Trent not to ban polyphonic music.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about choral music by Italian composers. For 10 points each:
parts: Written in honor of Alessandro Manzoni, this sacred choral work uses the pounding of the timpani and bass drum to depict thunder in its “Dies Irae” movement.
This Renaissance composer supposedly convinced the Church not to ban polyphony by writing the Pope Marcellus Mass.

Grigorio Allegri is most famous for this composition, a setting of Psalm 51 that is commonly performed during the Tenebrae service during holy week. Two alternating choirs generally sing this piece in falso bordone style.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer the following about choral music by Italian composers. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Written in honor of Alessandro Manzoni, this sacred choral work uses the pounding of the timpani and bass drum to depict thunder in its "Dies Irae" movement.
This Renaissance composer supposedly convinced the Church not to ban polyphony by writing the Pope Marcellus Mass.

Grigorio Allegri is most famous for this composition, a setting of Psalm 51 that is commonly performed during the Tenebrae service during holy week. Two alternating choirs generally sing this piece in falso bordone style.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about classical counterpoint, as taught by the hokey instructional dialogues in J.J. Fux’s treatise Gradus Ad Parnassum, for 10 points each.
parts: Fux divided counterpoint into this many “species” of increasing complexity and fluidity. In common-practice music, dominant chords build on this number’s scale degree, which is represented by “sol” in solfege.

Fux’s book taught counterpoint in the style of this man, who composed the six-voice motet “O Magnum Mysterium.” According to later legends, which are untrue, he saved polyphony with his Pope Marcellus Mass.
In a traditional Fuxian two-voice counterpoint exercise, this type of motion occurs when the top voice moves up or down but the cantus firmus stays on the same note.
answers_sanitized: five [or V; or 5; or fifth-species counterpoint]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer the following about classical counterpoint, as taught by the hokey instructional dialogues in J.J. Fux's treatise Gradus Ad Parnassum, for 10 points each.
parts_sanitized: Fux divided counterpoint into this many "species" of increasing complexity and fluidity. In common-practice music, dominant chords build on this number's scale degree, which is represented by "sol" in solfege.

Fux's book taught counterpoint in the style of this man, who composed the six-voice motet "O Magnum Mysterium." According to later legends, which are untrue, he saved polyphony with his Pope Marcellus Mass.
In a traditional Fuxian two-voice counterpoint exercise, this type of motion occurs when the top voice moves up or down but the cantus firmus stays on the same note.

bonuses.leadin: This composer spent the last two decades of his career as choirmaster of the Cappella Giulia. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Renaissance composer, whose Pope Marcellus Mass apocryphally convinced the Council of Trent not to ban polyphony.

The Pope Marcellus Mass is this type of mass, which borrows multiple voices from a pre-existing, usually secular, musical source. It contrasts with cantus firmus or paraphrase masses, in which only one voice is borrowed.
The first movement of an ordinary mass is traditionally a setting of this prayer in Greek, which asks for the Lord’s mercy. It is usually followed by a Gloria.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This composer spent the last two decades of his career as choirmaster of the Cappella Giulia. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this Renaissance composer, whose Pope Marcellus Mass apocryphally convinced the Council of Trent not to ban polyphony.

The Pope Marcellus Mass is this type of mass, which borrows multiple voices from a pre-existing, usually secular, musical source. It contrasts with cantus firmus or paraphrase masses, in which only one voice is borrowed.
The first movement of an ordinary mass is traditionally a setting of this prayer in Greek, which asks for the Lord's mercy. It is usually followed by a Gloria.

bonuses.leadin: The Venetian or polychoral style of this type of composition was pioneered by Giovanni Gabrieli and was continued by his student Heinrich Schütz in his Symphoniae sacrae. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this type of polyphonic vocal work, the sacred counterpart of the madrigal, examples of which include Tallis's Spem in alium and Byrd's Cantiones sacrae.
This Italian Renaissance composer wrote a collection of motets setting texts from the Song of Solomon, his Canticum canticorum. His Pope Marcellus Mass is credited with saving polyphony.
Mozart's final and most celebrated motet is a setting of this Latin text praising the true body of the Virgin Mary. Liszt combined this motet with Allegri's Miserere in his À la Chapelle Sixtine.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The Venetian or polychoral style of this type of composition was pioneered by Giovanni Gabrieli and was continued by his student Heinrich Schutz in his Symphoniae sacrae. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this type of polyphonic vocal work, the sacred counterpart of the madrigal, examples of which include Tallis's Spem in alium and Byrd's Cantiones sacrae.
This Italian Renaissance composer wrote a collection of motets setting texts from the Song of Solomon, his Canticum canticorum. His Pope Marcellus Mass is credited with saving polyphony.
Mozart's final and most celebrated motet is a setting of this Latin text praising the true body of the Virgin Mary. Liszt combined this motet with Allegri's Miserere in his A la Chapelle Sixtine.

bonuses.leadin: This composer’s very short Opus 36 Sonatina was parodied in an Erik Satie work titled Sonatine bureaucratique, and he also wrote the “Great National Symphony,” his third. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this composer, who is not Carl Czerny, who wrote instructional piano pieces in Gradus ad Parnassum.
Carl Czerny, who also wrote a Gradus ad Parnassum, was the teacher of this other composer. This man wrote some Transcendental Etudes as well as the Mephisto Waltzes and Hungarian Rhapsodies.
This other composer was used as an example in Fux’s Gradus ad Parnassum. He supposedly saved polyphony by writing the Pope Marcellus Mass.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This composer's very short Opus 36 Sonatina was parodied in an Erik Satie work titled Sonatine bureaucratique, and he also wrote the "Great National Symphony," his third. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this composer, who is not Carl Czerny, who wrote instructional piano pieces in Gradus ad Parnassum.
Carl Czerny, who also wrote a Gradus ad Parnassum, was the teacher of this other composer. This man wrote some Transcendental Etudes as well as the Mephisto Waltzes and Hungarian Rhapsodies.
This other composer was used as an example in Fux's Gradus ad Parnassum. He supposedly saved polyphony by writing the Pope Marcellus Mass.

bonuses.leadin: This composer believed dissonance should be confined to weak beats, according to a theory advocating him by Joseph Fux, for 10 points:
parts: Name this Renaissance composer and master of counterpoint whose works include a series of motets based on the Song of Solomon, an eight part setting of the Stabat Mater, and a Missa sina nomine that may have influenced Bach’s Mass in B Minor.
Palestrina is most famous for this work, which according to legend convinced the Council of Trent not to ban polyphonic settings of sacred text.
A notable school of polyphony was lead by composers like Leonin and Perotin and was named this. This is also the name of an early polyphonic mass by Guillame de Machaut.
answers_sanitized: Pope Marcellus Mass [or Missa Papae Marcelli]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This composer believed dissonance should be confined to weak beats, according to a theory advocating him by Joseph Fux, for 10 points:
parts_sanitized: Name this Renaissance composer and master of counterpoint whose works include a series of motets based on the Song of Solomon, an eight part setting of the Stabat Mater, and a Missa sina nomine that may have influenced Bach's Mass in B Minor.
Palestrina is most famous for this work, which according to legend convinced the Council of Trent not to ban polyphonic settings of sacred text.
A notable school of polyphony was lead by composers like Leonin and Perotin and was named this. This is also the name of an early polyphonic mass by Guillame de Machaut.

bonuses.leadin: Ferdinand I intervened to stop the movement to ban polyphony at this Church council. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this council of the Catholic Church, which did require that religious music no longer be based on existing secular melodies, and outlawed the use of vocal parts that could not be understood by parishioners.
Though Ferdinand’s quashing prevented the issue from even coming to a vote, an exaggerated legend states that this motet master saved polyphony at the Council of Trent with his Pope Marcellus Mass.
This twentieth-century composer of Krakow Greetings and The Rose Garden depicted the polyphony legend in his opera Palestrina.
